# Nydia M. Cappas (Psychology)

<!-- vimeo: 1111495849 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Hello, I'm Dr. Nidia Kafa. I am a clinical psychology professor at Poncy Health Sciences University in Puerto Rico. And I've been teaching for almost 20 years and since the long, most creative, fun, and innovative years of my teaching career. So today I want to share with you some of the things that I've done to transform my classes. What do I teach? So within the clinical psychology program, I teach a couple of courses. One of them is program development, and the other courses are practicum courses that are designed to teach our students very practical clinical skills. And it's within one of these, um, courses that I had the challenge of how do I make sure that my students are learning the skills and competencies that I'm teaching, even in the age of ai. So whereas before I would've been able to ask them to write a paper or a conceptualization, now those are tasks that are too easily to de too easy to delegate to ai. And so I wanna make sure that I have some activities and assessments that are not easily delegated to ai, but also some that can include AI in them. And so my process is that first I identify what are those non-negotiables, those skills and competencies that I need to make sure that students have. Sometimes they have to do with writing, but sometimes they don't. So once I figure that out, I then start brainstorming on different ways in which I can assess and practice them with and without ai. And then I go to work and I build the experiences and I test them with my students. I am very open about using AI and experimenting, and I often see it as a journey that I'm doing together with my students. And that has been very helpful. So in that context, what I'm sharing with you, it's a custom GPT that I built to help my students learn about motivational interviewing. So motivational interviewing is a therapeutic approach that help people do the changes that they need to do to be more healthy or, or feel better. And, uh, that often comes even when people are not ready to change. And so the patient I've built is a patient in a pre-contemplation stage of change. That's the stage where we're not even aware or see that we need to change. And so that tends to be really challenging. The GPTE, the custom GPT serves as the patient, acts as a patient and engages in a conversation. And so it gives my students the chance to practice with the patient and do the questions and all the techniques that are necessary in a very safe environment. And great because this students can do this even regardless of their Chad GPT access. So they can have the free version of chat GPT, and they can use the custom GPT that I built because they don't need to. And so that has been, um, very helpful. The way that I've used it is that I start within the context of the class. 'cause I want to make sure that everyone understands the task and that they're doing well. And, um, I get feedback and students have very precise questions because they're actually practicing the skill in a very safe, uh, way. And I also use that as assessment when students can have that conversation in the, with me, the link to the chat or the PDF that they can produce, uh, of the chat. And that gives me a very good chance to assess very specifically, what are the things that I need to approach or tackle in the context of class so that they can. So this has been very helpful. Prompt that I use is very easy. I built it with chat GBT in a conversational way where I told it what I wanted my students to have and it helped me build it. I tested a couple of times until I got exactly what I wanted. And it has been great how it has gone. The feedback has been phenomenal. I think student engagement classes are very fun and I think that my openness, uh, to explore AI has also helped my students open and share what they are finding with me. Like the case of one of my students who did a salsa song for, to study it for one of his tests, I think it was anxiety disorders. And he built a salsa song about anxiety disorders. And so they feel very open to share with this with me. And I think this has contributed to a very, to a great learning environment. I think also the ability for me to evaluate the skills, like in the case of the chat that I built, has been very good. And for the most part that those changes that I have done in assessment and classes have been very helpful both for the students but also for me to assess, uh, how the students are learning in the course. And then also it's the time, um, management thing that I think that AI has been so helpful. I, my, I spend my commute talking with, um, voice mode, um, with advanced voice mode. And I am usually, this is really fun and it's, um, contributed to some of the fun things that I've done this past couple of years. And also the fact that I've been able to share some of these with my colleagues and professors and they in turn share what they're learning with me. So that's also been, I think, a fun experience. So that's my take on ai. I really think that AI can be a tool for a great tool for teaching and for learning, but it depends a lot on how we approach it because I am also aware that it can hinder learning. And so to me it's critical that as professors, we are seated at the table where decisions are being made. We need to contribute, especially those who are worried, they need to engage with AI and with child GPT and understand it deeply so that we can then say what is gonna be for, what things are, is AI good? And what are the things we need to, to not use ai? So we need more professors at the table. And so, uh, that's why I am doing this and I am very happy and thankful for Open AI for the invitation to share this experience with all of you. Thank you.
